_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 10-2 Chemical Exposure (MDHHS Reviewed 2023-05-09). Base Michigan template.
protocol_reference: Michigan Special Operations Protocol Section 10-2: Chemical Exposure — MDHHS Reviewed 2023
purpose: Provide EMS treatment guidelines for patients exposed to non-nerve agent chemical agents including choking agents, vesicants/blister agents, and lacrimators/riot control agents after decontamination.

sections.title: General Principles — All Chemical Exposures
content: DO NOT enter contaminated area without appropriate PPE — refer to Protocol 10-1 for PPE levels
Decontamination is required before EMS treatment — gross decon performed by fire/HazMat in warm zone
Remove and bag all clothing — removes approximately 80% of surface contamination
Copious water irrigation for all skin/eye exposures — minimum 15 minutes continuous irrigation
Notify online medical control and receiving hospital of agent type and number of patients
Supportive care is the cornerstone of treatment for most non-nerve agent chemical exposures

sections.title: Choking / Pulmonary Agents (Chlorine, Phosgene, Diphosgene)
content: Agents: Chlorine (yellow-green gas, bleach odor), Phosgene (colorless gas, freshly cut grass odor), Diphosgene
Mechanism: mucosal irritation → pulmonary edema (delayed 2-24hr with phosgene)
Signs/Symptoms: coughing, choking, dyspnea, chest tightness, eye/nasal irritation; may progress to severe pulmonary edema
WARNING: Phosgene — delayed pulmonary edema; patient may appear well initially then deteriorate rapidly
TREATMENT:
— Remove patient from exposure; decontaminate (water irrigation)
— High-flow oxygen via NRB mask or BVM as needed
— If bronchospasm present: nebulized albuterol (see MCA selection below)
— CPAP for severe respiratory distress with adequate respiratory rate
— Eye irrigation with water or NS minimum 15 minutes for eye exposure
— Tetracaine ophthalmic drops to facilitate irrigation if MCA authorized (Protocol 9-43R)
— Do NOT induce vomiting; treat symptomatically
— Monitor continuously for delayed pulmonary edema; transport all patients for evaluation
— All phosgene-exposed patients must be observed minimum 24hr — warn receiving hospital

sections.title: Vesicants / Blister Agents (Mustard, Lewisite, Phosgene Oxime)
content: Agents: Sulfur mustard (HD — garlic/mustard odor), Lewisite (L — geraniums odor), Phosgene oxime (CX — sharp penetrating odor)
Mechanism: alkylation of DNA/cellular structures → delayed blistering; Lewisite = immediate vesication
Signs/Symptoms:
— Mustard: delayed 2-24hr onset; blistering, eye irritation, laryngeal edema, bone marrow suppression
— Lewisite: immediate pain, blistering, eye/airway irritation
— Phosgene Oxime: immediate pain/urticaria; coagulative tissue necrosis
TREATMENT:
— IMMEDIATE decontamination is critical — remove clothing, copious water irrigation
— High-flow oxygen; support airway — laryngeal edema may require advanced airway
— Treat burns symptomatically — do NOT puncture blisters in prehospital setting
— Eye irrigation minimum 15 minutes; tetracaine if MCA authorized
— Ventilate as needed; CPAP for pulmonary edema
— Do NOT apply ointments or dressings that trap contamination
— Transport all exposed patients — delayed effects are common

sections.title: Lacrimators / Riot Control Agents (CS, CN, OC/Pepper Spray)
content: Agents: CS (o-chlorobenzylidene malononitrile), CN (chloroacetophenone), OC (oleoresin capsicum/pepper spray), Mace
Mechanism: sensory nerve irritation — mucosal/skin irritation; NOT typically life-threatening in open air
Signs/Symptoms: intense tearing/eye pain, rhinorrhea, coughing, skin burning, blepharospasm; resolves minutes to hours after decontamination
TREATMENT:
— Move patient to fresh air; remove from exposure
— Remove and bag contaminated clothing
— Copious water irrigation for skin and eyes
— Eye irrigation minimum 15 minutes for significant eye exposure; tetracaine if MCA authorized to facilitate irrigation
— Most patients resolve with decontamination and fresh air alone
— Oxygen for significant respiratory symptoms
— Bronchospasm: nebulized albuterol (see MCA selection below)
— Severe persistent symptoms or enclosed-space exposure: transport for evaluation
— Warn receiving hospital of chemical used (OC, CS, CN)

sections.title: Nerve Agent / Organophosphate Exposure
content: REFER TO PROTOCOL 10-3 — Nerve Agent / Organophosphate Exposure
Indicators: SLUDGEM (salivation, lacrimation, urination, defecation, GI distress, emesis, miosis), bronchospasm, seizures
Do NOT attempt to treat nerve agent exposure under this protocol — use Protocol 10-3

sections.title: Cyanide / Blood Agent Exposure
content: REFER TO PROTOCOL 10-5 — Cyanide Exposure
Indicators: rapid loss of consciousness, seizures, normal or pink skin color despite hypoxia, normal SpO2 with severe distress
Do NOT attempt to treat cyanide exposure under this protocol — use Protocol 10-5

mca_selections_required.label: Nebulized Albuterol — EMT authorization for bronchospasm in chemical exposure
mca_selections_required.description: MCA selection authorizes EMT-level providers to administer nebulized albuterol for bronchospasm resulting from chemical agent exposure (choking agents, lacrimators). Without MCA selection, nebulized albuterol at EMT level is not authorized.

key_drugs: Albuterol (nebulized) — bronchospasm from choking agents or lacrimators (MCA selection required for EMT)
Oxygen — all chemical exposures with respiratory symptoms
Tetracaine ophthalmic — facilitate eye irrigation (MCA Optional per Protocol 9-43R)
deterioration_flags: Laryngeal edema or progressive stridor — prepare for advanced airway management
Delayed pulmonary edema (especially phosgene) — may develop 2-24hr post-exposure; all phosgene patients require hospital observation
Loss of consciousness, seizures, or severe hemodynamic compromise — consider concurrent nerve agent or cyanide exposure; refer to Protocol 10-3 or 10-5
scope_notes: This protocol covers chemical exposures excluding nerve agents (Protocol 10-3) and cyanide (Protocol 10-5). Decontamination must occur before EMS treatment — do NOT transport contaminated patients. Coordinate with HazMat and online medical control for all chemical exposure events.
